lesson.menu_title: Respectfully, No -- Disagreeing With a Stakeholder
lesson.menu_desc: Say no to power: concern, evidence, downside, alternative -- 29 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 11 -- Respectfully, No -- Disagreeing With a Stakeholder
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Diogo Leal | Aula 11 -- Respectfully, No

plan_row.tema: Respectfully, No -- Disagreeing With a Stakeholder
plan_row.foco: Polite disagreement: I see your point, but... + because + What if we...?
plan_row.atividade: Disagreement note + role-play with a stakeholder who does not want to hear it
plan_row.homework: Record a 2-min disagreement: concern, evidence, downside, alternative

bank.label: How to disagree and keep the room
items: I see your point, and I understand the client.
But I have one concern about the timing.
In March, the same decision cost us three weeks.
The downside is two weeks of onboarding, taken out of testing.
What if we give them the reports instead?
If you still want the integration, I need two extra weeks in the plan.
Your stakeholder wants to cut the test phase in half to hit the date. You think it is dangerous.
A director decides your team will use a new tool in the middle of the project. Nobody knows it.
The client asks for a feature that will break the integration. Your boss says yes before asking you.

items.situation: Your director says: 'This is decided. Any objections?' You have one. Speak.
tips: Soften first: 'I see your point, but...'

items.situation: Say your concern in one sentence, with no adjectives.
tips: 'I have one concern about the timing.'

items.situation: Give the evidence -- a fact from a past project.
tips: A date, a number, a result.

items.situation: Name the downside of her idea, without attacking her.
tips: Attack the plan, never the person.
Make it easy to say yes.

items.situation: She keeps her decision. Close the conversation without losing her.
tips: Ask for what you need to make it work.

pressure.title: Answer live
items: Are you telling me the client is wrong?
Why did you not raise this three weeks ago?
If I say no to your alternative, will you still support the decision in front of the team?

pressure.text: Answer first, evidence second, alternative third. Never attack the person -- only the plan.

listenings.text: Diogo, quick one before the meeting. I have decided to bring a new vendor into the project. They start on Monday, and they will take the integration work. I know it is late in the project, and I know your team is used to working alone. But the client is nervous, and this makes them feel safe. I am not asking for a debate in the meeting. If you have a concern, bring it to me now, with evidence, and bring me an alternative. What I will not accept is a long list of problems and no solution. And Diogo, do not go quiet in the meeting and complain later.

Sophia, I see your point, and I understand why the client feels safer. But I have one concern, and I want to be direct about it. In March we brought a vendor in during the test phase, and the integration took three extra weeks. That is my evidence. The downside here is the same: a new team needs two weeks just to understand our system, and those two weeks come out of testing. I am not saying no. I would suggest an alternative: give them the reports, not the integration. They start on Monday, the client sees a new team, and my team keeps the part that is risky. If the reports go well, we give them more in July. I think that is worth it. If you still want them on the integration, I will make it work, but I want the extra two weeks in the plan.

extra_audio.text: Sophia announces that a new vendor starts on Monday, on the integration. Diogo softens, and says he has one concern. He gives his evidence: in March, the same decision cost three extra weeks. He names the downside: two weeks of onboarding, taken out of testing. Finally, he offers an alternative: the vendor takes the reports, and his team keeps the integration.
